content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 8, 2018


Eric Lucan
Councilmember, City of Novato
26 Truman Drive
Novato, CA 94947

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-208

Dear Mr. Lucan:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are, when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions
should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

     Does the Act prohibit you, a Novato City Councilmember, from taking part in governmental
decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater
Group to complete the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is
located within approximately 300 feet of a residential rental property owned by you and your wife?

CONCLUSION

     Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions because they would have a
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your real property interest in your residential rental
property under Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).

FACTS

     The Novato Theater Group, a 501(c)(3) organization, owns the Novato Theater, a historic
former movie house located in the City of Novato's downtown that has been shuttered since 1991.
The Novato Theater Group has been reconstructing the Novato Theater for use as a community

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise stated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-208
Page No. 2

theater, and recently had to halt the ongoing reconstruction due to lack of funding. The Novato City
Council will soon consider whether to provide financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group to
enable the reconstruction of the Novato Theater to proceed. The Novato Theater will be a 250-seat
community theater once the reconstruction is complete.

You are a City Councilmember, and you and your wife own a residential duplex located on
Machin Avenue, within approximately 300 feet of the Novato Theater. The duplex has on-site
parking reserved for your tenants, but there is only limited street parking nearby the Novato
Theater, and the facts presented provide no indication of new parking facilities intended to serve the
Novato Theater. The area surrounding the Novato Theater is zoned commercial, but your duplex is
a permitted non-conforming use because it predates the implementation the commercial zoning of
the area. You purchased the duplex in 2014, after previous City commitments to provide funding to
the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, but prior to your election to
the City Council. You own another residential rental property located in different part of the City.

On September 10, 2018, you requested formal written advice regarding whether the Act's
conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in the decisions relating to the City's
potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for reconstruction of the
Novato Theater. You provided additional information by phone on October 5, 2018.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of an official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public
generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying
conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103. Of those interests, your interests in
the decisions at issue based on the facts presented are as follows:

• A real property interest in you and your wife's duplex, assuming you have an interest of $2,000
or more in the duplex. (See Section 87103(b).)

• A business interest in your residential rental property management business, assuming either
you have an investment in the business worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or you
hold any position of management in the business (see Section 87103(d)).

• A source of income interest in your residential rental property management business, and in any
tenants of your residential rental properties, assuming you aggregate $500 or more from each
within the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. (See Section 87103(c).)

• A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate
family members. (See Section 87103.)

Foreseeability

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in

File No. A-18-208
Page No. 3

the decision, and that an interest is explicitly involved if the official's interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect of the decision on an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if the "effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."

None of your interests are explicitly involved in the decisions at issue. Therefore, those decisions' effect on each of your interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility, more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Materiality

Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(11), a decision's effect on an official's real property interest, other than an interest in commercial property containing a business, is material if the decision affects real property value located within 500 feet of the official's property, unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that the decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. Regulation 18702.2(a)(11) applies to an official's interest in residential rental property. (Strong Advice Letter, No. A-18-111; Berger Advice Letter, No. A-18-108; Malibie Advice Letter, No. 1-17-278.)

Your duplex is located approximately 300 feet from the Novato Theater. The Novato Theater has been shuttered since 1991, when the building ceased operating as a movie house. If the ongoing reconstruction of the Novato Theater resumes due to the City's provision of financial assistance, it will be substantially more likely that the reconstruction will be completed and that the building will begin being used as a 250-seat community theatre.

The Novato Theater's reopening and use as a community theatre will increase the intensity of use of the surrounding area, including traffic and demand for parking. The facts presented provide no indication of new parking facilities intended to serve the Novato Theater. Although your duplex has dedicated on-site parking for tenants, guests of your tenants would be less likely to find available street parking nearby the duplex when there is an event at the Novato Theater because of increased demand.

Furthermore, your duplex is unique within the surrounding area because it is a residential property that is a permitted non-conforming use within a commercial zone. This status makes the duplex one of the few residential properties in close proximity to the Novato Theatre. An operating community theater that replaces a vacant building within 300 feet of your duplex could add to the market value of the duplex, especially in light of the duplex being one of very few residential properties located nearby the theater.

Conversely, there is not sufficient evidence indicating there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your property.

File No. A-18-208
Page No. 4

Therefore, we conclude that the decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group to complete the reconstruction of the Novato Theater would have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your real property interest in your duplex under Regulation 18702.2(a)(11). Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[Signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

² We do not analyze your other financial interests with respect to the decisions at issue because we have already determined that the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit you, a Novato City Councilmember, from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater 
Group to complete the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is 
located within approximately 300 feet of a residential rental property owned by you and your wife? 
CdNCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions because they would have a 
reasoqably foreseeable measurable impact on your real property interest in your residential rental 
property under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1 ).

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or 
more of an official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public 
generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying 
conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103. Of those interests, your interests in

• 
A real property interest in you and your wife's duplex, assuming you have an interest of $2,000 
or more in the duplex. (See Section 87103(b).) 
• 
A business interest in your residential rental property management business, assuming either 
you have an investment in the business worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or you 
hold any position of management in the business (see Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A source of income interest in your residential rental property management business, and in any 
tenants of your residential rental properties, assuming you aggregate $500 or more from each 
within the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. (See Section 87103(c).) 
• 
A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate 
family members. (See Section 87103.)

Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in  
the decision, and that an interest is explicitly involved if the official's interest is a named party in, 
or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 18701 (b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable 
to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect of 
the decision on an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if the "effect can 
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
foreseeable." 
None of your interests are explicitly involved in the decisions at issue. Therefore, those 
decisions' effect on each of your interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility, more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Materiality 
Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1), a decision's effect on an official's real property interest, 
other than an interest in commercial property containing a business, is material if the decision 
affects real property value located within 500 feet of the official's property, unless there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that the decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable 
impact on the official's property. Regulation 18702.2(a)(l l) applies to an official's interest in 
residential rental property. (Strong Advice Letter, No. A-18-111; Berger Advice Letter, No. A-18-
108; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. I-17-278.) 
Your duplex is located approximately 300 feet from the Novato Theater. The Novato 
Theater has been shuttered since 1991, when the building ceased operating as a movie house. If the 
ongoing reconstruction of the Novato Theater resumes due to the City's provision of financial

building will begin being used as a 250-seat community theatre.

of use of the surrounding area, including traffic and demand for parking. The facts presented

available streetparking nearby the duplex when there is an event at the Novato Theater because of

properties located nearby the theater. 
Conversely, there is not sufficient evidence indicating there will be no reasonably 
foreseeable measurable impact on your property.  
Therefore, we conclude that the decisions relating to the City's potential provision of 
financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group to complete the reconstruction of the Novato 
Theater would have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your real property interest in 
· your duplex under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1 ). Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in those
